narrative: As a white male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, predicting their likelihood of passing on the first try. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 32, top-decile performance in Year 1 and Year 3 of school, and plans to attend full-time—and estimated a 40.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students predicted to pass with high confidence. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 school decile ranking, your Year 1 school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA, in that order of impact. Your exceptional Year 3 decile of 10—far above the typical student's average of around 5.5—strongly pushed the prediction toward you passing the bar, signaling outstanding performance in your later school years. Similarly, your top Year 1 decile of 10, well above the average of about 5.7, worked in your favor, reinforcing the model's view of your academic potential early on. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, which is below the dataset average of 3.21, had the opposite effect among these top factors, nudging the prediction toward failure and offsetting some of those strong school showings. Compared to typical students, your LSAT of 32 falls short of the average 36.7, but it wasn't as pivotal here, while your full-time status aligns with the vast majority at 92.7%. Overall, while your recent school deciles shine brighter than most, the model weighed your lower undergrad GPA heavily enough to tip the balance toward concern about bar success. This doesn't define your potential, especially as a determined applicant from a middle-income background who's already proven top-tier performance in school. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT or undergrad-level courses to boost your GPA, gathering strong recommendation letters highlighting your Year 3 turnaround, or applying to programs with more holistic reviews that value your full-time commitment and recent achievements. With targeted improvements, you can reshape this narrative and strengthen your next application.
